content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
August 12,
1992
Honorable Tom Bates
California State Assembly
State Capitol,
Room 446
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1-92-319
Dear Assemblyman Bates:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
/
Since you seek general guidance regarding
reporting requirements,
we are treating this as a request for
informal assistance.
2 /
You have asked what method should be used to determine how to
report the value of campaign literature which advocates particular
positions or recommendations on thirteen local ballot measures and
candidates.
The cost of the design, printing and postage of the
mailer is approximately $2,400.
Pursuant to a telephone
conversation with Elizabeth Jewel of your staff on July 23,
1992,
you consulted with some of the candidates or measures regarding
the mailer.
The Act requires that committees disclose, at specified
times, all contributions and other receipts,
and all expenditures.
(Section 84211.)
Generally, all costs related to campaign
literature are reportable.
(Kawaqoe Advice Letter,
A—81-173.)
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory refer
ences are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Com
mission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq.
All references to regulations are to Title
..2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 183:29(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-92—319
Page
2
The Act defines a contribution,
in part,
as follows:
“Contribution” means a payment,
a forgiveness
of a loan,
a payment of a loan by a third party,
or
an enforceable promise to make a payment except to
the extent that full and adequate consideration is
received unless it is clear from the surrounding
circumstances that it is not made for political
purposes.
An expenditure made at the behest of a
candidate, committee or elected officer is a
contribution to the candidate,
committee or elected
officer unless full and adequate consideration is
received for making the expenditure.
Section 82015
(Emphasis added.)
“Made at the behest” means a payment made under the control
or at the direction of,
in cooperation, consultation,
coordination, or concert with,
or at the request or suggestion of
a candidate, controlled committee, official committee of a
political party,
or organization formed or existing primarily for
political purposes.
(Regulation 18215(b).)
Thus, expenditures
made at the behest of a candidate or committee are reportable as
contributions. 3/
The standard for valuing nonmonetary contributions to
candidates or to committees is explicit.
“Whenever the amount of
goods, services, facilities, or anything of value other than money
is required to be reported under this title, the amount reported
shall be the fair market value....”
(Section 82025.5.)
The
Commission has interpreted this to mean the amount that would have
to be paid by the candidate or committee to acquire similar goods
or services on the open market.
(White Advice Letter,
No.
A—77-
08-018.)
We suggest the following method for determining the
value of the nonmonetary contributions received by these
candidates or measures:
(1)
Divide the total cost of producing the mailer by the
number produced.
(2)
Multiply that figure by the number of mailers sent on
behalf of that candidate or measure.
However,
expenditures in connection with campaign literature
which advocate a particular position on a ballot measure are not
noninonetary contributions or independent expenditures for or
against a ballot measure if taking a position on significant
public issues, or urging others to take a position, is an integral
part of the candidate’s own campaign.
(Dascenzo Advice Letter,
A—82—082.)


Our File No.
1-92-319
Page
3
Thus, if you produced 1,000 mailers at a total cost of
$2,400, but only 450 mailers were sent in candidate A’s district,
you would divide $2,400 by 1,000 and multiply that figure by 450.
This would mean that a total of $1,080 is attributable to that
candidate.
However, if all the mailers were sent in that
candidate’s district, the total amount of $2,400 would be
attributable to that candidate.
The Act also provides in Section 82031:
“Independent expenditure” means an expenditure
made by any person in connection with a
communication which expressly advocates the
election or defeat of a clearly identified
candidate or the qualification, passage or defeat
of a clearly identified measure,
or taken as a
whole and in context, unambiguously urges a
particular result in an election but which is not
made to or at the behest of the affected candidate
or committee.
Consequently, the expenditures not made at the behest of a
candidate or committee are reportable independent expenditures.
(Section 84203.5.)
You may determine the costs attributable to
each candidate or measure on a pro—rata basis
($2,400 divided by
13)
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—59O1./
Sincerely,
Luisa Menéhaca
Counsel, Legal Division
LM: im
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of lO per page.

sections.question: What method should be used to determine how to report the value of campaign literature which advocates particular positions or recommendations on thirteen local ballot measures and candidates, where the cost of design, printing and postage of the mailer is approximately $2,400?
sections.conclusion: The standard method for valuing nonmonetary contributions is the fair market value, which is the amount that would have to be paid to acquire similar goods or services on the open market. The FPPC suggests dividing the total cost of producing the mailer by the number produced, then multiplying that figure by the number of mailers sent on behalf of each candidate or measure. Alternatively, costs may be allocated on a pro-rata basis ($2,400 divided by 13). Expenditures in connection with campaign literature advocating a position on a ballot measure are not nonmonetary contributions if taking a position on public issues is an integral part of the candidate's own campaign.
sections.question_synthetic: How should an assemblyman report the value of campaign literature promoting thirteen local ballot measures and candidates when the total production cost is $2,400?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises using fair market value methodology, calculated by dividing total mailer cost by quantity produced and multiplying by the number sent per candidate/measure, or alternatively using pro-rata allocation. Expenditures for campaign literature on ballot measures may not be reportable as contributions if advocating positions is integral to the candidate's campaign.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an informal advice letter (not formal opinion, so limited immunity per note 2). Document contains clear question and conclusion sections. References to specific advice letters and statutes included. Telephone consultation with staff occurred July 23, 1992 prior to written request.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What method should be used to determine how to report the value of campaign literature which advocates particular positions or recommendations on thirteen local ballot measures and candidates, where the cost of design, printing and postage of the mailer is approximately $2,400?

CONCLUSION: The standard method for valuing nonmonetary contributions is the fair market value, which is the amount that would have to be paid to acquire similar goods or services on the open market. The FPPC suggests dividing the total cost of producing the mailer by the number produced, then multiplying that figure by the number of mailers sent on behalf of each candidate or measure. Alternatively, costs may be allocated on a pro-rata basis ($2,400 divided by 13). Expenditures in connection with campaign literature advocating a position on a ballot measure are not nonmonetary contributions if taking a position on public issues is an integral part of the candidate's own campaign.

embedding.summary: The FPPC provides informal advice to Assemblyman Tom Bates on reporting requirements for campaign literature promoting thirteen local ballot measures and candidates, explaining fair market value calculation methods and distinguishing between reportable contributions and independent expenditures.
